---
title: "Wooldridge Introductory Econometrics -- Ch. 6: Multiple regression: further issues (via StatEcon)"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

**Source.** Stata examples reproduced from Wooldridge, *Introductory Econometrics: A Modern Approach* (Cengage). Data are the book's companion files.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

A few small helpers reproduce Stata's post-estimation tools from the model that
`stata_regress` returns. `bget` pulls a coefficient by name; `stdbeta` reports
Stata's standardized (`, beta`) coefficients; `predict_ci` reproduces
`predict, stdp`/`stdf` for a hypothetical observation `x0` (in coefficient
order, intercept first).

```{julia}
cn(m)      = string.(StatsBase.coefnames(m))
bvec(m)    = StatsBase.coef(m)
Vmat(m)    = StatsBase.vcov(m)
bget(m, s) = bvec(m)[findfirst(==(s), cn(m))]

# Stata's `, beta`: b_j * sd(x_j)/sd(y) over the estimation sample.
function stdbeta(df, y, xs)
    sy = Statistics.std(df[!, y])
    m  = stata_regress(df; y = y, x = xs)
    for x in xs
        b = bget(m, string(x))
        Printf.@printf("  %-10s beta = % .7g\n", x, b * Statistics.std(df[!, x]) / sy)
    end
    return m
end

# Point prediction and its SE for a new x0 (coefficient order, intercept first).
# forecast=true adds sigma^2 (Stata's stdf); otherwise mean-prediction SE (stdp).
function predict_ci(m, x0; forecast = false, sigma2 = nothing)
    b = bvec(m); V = Vmat(m)
    xb   = LinearAlgebra.dot(b, x0)
    varp = LinearAlgebra.dot(x0, V * x0)
    forecast && (varp += sigma2)
    sd = sqrt(varp)
    Printf.@printf("  xb = %.7g   se = %.7g\n  95%% CI = [%.7g, %.7g]\n",
                   xb, sd, xb - 1.96sd, xb + 1.96sd)
    return (xb = xb, se = sd)
end

# Residual variance sigma^2 = RSS / dof_residual (Stata's Residual MS).
function sigma2_of(m, df, y)
    r = df[!, y] .- FixedEffectModels.predict(m, df)
    rr = collect(skipmissing(r))
    sum(abs2, rr) / StatsBase.dof_residual(m)
end
```

## Example 6.1: Effect of Pollution on Housing Prices

```{julia}
#=
use "data/hprice2.dta", clear
reg price nox crime rooms dist stratio, beta

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =       506
-------------+----------------------------------   F(5, 500)       =    174.47
       Model |  2.7223e+10         5  5.4445e+09   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  1.5603e+10       500  31205611.6   R-squared       =    0.6357
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.6320
       Total |  4.2826e+10       505    84803032   Root MSE        =    5586.2

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
       price | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|                     Beta
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         nox |  -2706.433   354.0869    -7.64   0.000                 -.340446
       crime |   -153.601   32.92883    -4.66   0.000                -.1432828
       rooms |   6735.498   393.6037    17.11   0.000                 .5138878
        dist |  -1026.806   188.1079    -5.46   0.000                -.2348385
     stratio |  -1149.204   127.4287    -9.02   0.000                -.2702799
       _cons |   20871.13   5054.599     4.13   0.000                        .
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
hprice2 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("hprice2"))
m61 = stata_regress(hprice2; y = :price, x = [:nox, :crime, :rooms, :dist, :stratio])
```

```{julia}
# Standardized (beta) coefficients, Stata's `, beta` column.
stdbeta(hprice2, :price, [:nox, :crime, :rooms, :dist, :stratio]);
```

The unstandardized fit matches Stata exactly (`nox = -2706.433`, `R² = 0.6357`,
`F(5,500) = 174.47`). The standardized coefficients also reproduce the published
`Beta` column (`nox = -.340446`, `rooms = .5138878`, `stratio = -.2702799`).

## Example 6.2: Effect of Pollution on Housing Prices

```{julia}
#=
gen rooms2=rooms*rooms
gen ldist=log(dist)
reg lprice lnox ldist rooms rooms2 stratio

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =       506
-------------+----------------------------------   F(5, 500)       =    151.77
       Model |  50.9872375         5  10.1974475   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  33.5949875       500  .067189975   R-squared       =    0.6028
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.5988
       Total |   84.582225       505  .167489554   Root MSE        =    .25921

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      lprice | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        lnox |   -.901682   .1146869    -7.86   0.000     -1.12701   -.6763544
       ldist |  -.0867814   .0432807    -2.01   0.045    -.1718159    -.001747
       rooms |  -.5451128   .1654542    -3.29   0.001    -.8701839   -.2200417
      rooms2 |   .0622612    .012805     4.86   0.000      .037103    .0874194
     stratio |  -.0475902   .0058542    -8.13   0.000     -.059092   -.0360884
       _cons |   13.38548   .5664732    23.63   0.000     12.27252    14.49844
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
hprice2.rooms2 = hprice2.rooms .* hprice2.rooms
hprice2.ldist  = log.(hprice2.dist)
m62 = stata_regress(hprice2; y = :lprice,
                     x = [:lnox, :ldist, :rooms, :rooms2, :stratio])
```

```{julia}
#=
Turnaround value of rooms
display -1*_b[rooms]/(2*_b[rooms2])
4.3776293
Change in price if rooms increases from 5 to 6
display 100*(_b[rooms]+2*_b[rooms2]*5)
7.7499072
Change in price if rooms increases from 6 to 7
display 100*(_b[rooms]+2*_b[rooms2]*6)
20.202144
=#
br = bget(m62, "rooms"); br2 = bget(m62, "rooms2")
Printf.@printf("turnaround rooms      = %.7g\n", -br / (2br2))
Printf.@printf("pct change 5 -> 6     = %.7g\n", 100 * (br + 2br2 * 5))
Printf.@printf("pct change 6 -> 7     = %.7g\n", 100 * (br + 2br2 * 6))
```

The quadratic-in-`rooms` fit matches Stata to the printed digits
(`rooms = -.5451128`, `rooms2 = .0622612`, `R² = 0.6028`). The turnaround value
`4.3776293`, and the marginal effects `7.7499072` (5→6) and `20.202144` (6→7),
all reproduce the published numbers.

## Example 6.3: Effect of Attendance on Final Exam Performance

```{julia}
#=
use "data/attend.dta", clear
summ priGPA

    Variable |        Obs        Mean    Std. dev.       Min        Max
-------------+---------------------------------------------------------
      priGPA |        680    2.586775    .5447141       .857       3.93
=#
attend = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("attend"))
Printf.@printf("Obs=%d  Mean=%.7g  Std=%.7g  Min=%.7g  Max=%.7g\n",
    length(attend.priGPA), Statistics.mean(attend.priGPA),
    Statistics.std(attend.priGPA), minimum(attend.priGPA), maximum(attend.priGPA))
```

```{julia}
#=
gen priGPA2=priGPA*priGPA
gen ACT2=ACT*ACT
gen priatn=priGPA*atndrte
reg stndfnl atndrte priGPA ACT priGPA2 ACT2 priatn

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =       680
-------------+----------------------------------   F(6, 673)       =     33.25
       Model |  152.001032         6  25.3335053   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  512.762536       673  .761905701   R-squared       =    0.2287
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.2218
       Total |  664.763568       679  .979033237   Root MSE        =    .87287

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     stndfnl | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     atndrte |  -.0067129   .0102321    -0.66   0.512    -.0268035    .0133777
      priGPA |   -1.62854   .4810025    -3.39   0.001    -2.572986   -.6840939
         ACT |  -.1280394    .098492    -1.30   0.194     -.321428    .0653492
     priGPA2 |   .2959046   .1010495     2.93   0.004     .0974945    .4943148
        ACT2 |   .0045334   .0021764     2.08   0.038       .00026    .0088068
      priatn |   .0055859   .0043174     1.29   0.196    -.0028913    .0140631
       _cons |   2.050293   1.360319     1.51   0.132    -.6206863    4.721272
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
attend.priGPA2 = attend.priGPA .* attend.priGPA
attend.ACT2    = attend.ACT    .* attend.ACT
attend.priatn  = attend.priGPA .* attend.atndrte
m63 = stata_regress(attend; y = :stndfnl,
                     x = [:atndrte, :priGPA, :ACT, :priGPA2, :ACT2, :priatn])
```

```{julia}
#=
Partial effect of atndrte on stndfnl
display _b[atndrte]+_b[priatn]*2.59
.00775457
=#
Printf.@printf("partial effect at priGPA=2.59: %.7g\n",
               bget(m63, "atndrte") + bget(m63, "priatn") * 2.59)
```

The summary of `priGPA` (mean `2.586775`, sd `.5447141`) and the full
interaction/quadratic regression match Stata to the printed digits
(`priGPA = -1.62854`, `priGPA2 = .2959046`, `R² = 0.2287`). The partial effect
of `atndrte` evaluated at the mean `priGPA` is `.00775457`, reproducing Stata.

## Example 6.4: CEO Compensation and Firm Performance

```{julia}
#=
use "data/ceosal1.dta", clear
reg salary sales roe

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =       209
-------------+----------------------------------   F(2, 206)       =      3.09
       Model |  11427511.8         2  5713755.89   Prob > F        =    0.0474
    Residual |   380305470       206  1846143.06   R-squared       =    0.0292
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.0197
       Total |   391732982       208  1883331.64   Root MSE        =    1358.7

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      salary | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       sales |   .0163416   .0088736     1.84   0.067    -.0011532    .0338363
         roe |   19.63097   11.07655     1.77   0.078     -2.20697    41.46891
       _cons |   830.6313   223.9049     3.71   0.000     389.1924     1272.07
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
ceosal1 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("ceosal1"))
m64a = stata_regress(ceosal1; y = :salary, x = [:sales, :roe])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg lsalary lsales roe

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =       209
-------------+----------------------------------   F(2, 206)       =     40.45
       Model |  18.8148991         2  9.40744953   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  47.9072642       206  .232559535   R-squared       =    0.2820
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.2750
       Total |  66.7221632       208  .320779631   Root MSE        =    .48224

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     lsalary | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      lsales |   .2750874    .033254     8.27   0.000     .2095257    .3406491
         roe |   .0178723   .0039551     4.52   0.000     .0100746    .0256699
       _cons |   4.362167   .2938776    14.84   0.000     3.782774    4.941561
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m64b = stata_regress(ceosal1; y = :lsalary, x = [:lsales, :roe])
```

Both the level (`sales = .0163416`, `R² = 0.0292`) and log-log
(`lsales = .2750874`, `roe = .0178723`, `R² = 0.2820`) specifications reproduce
the published Stata tables exactly.

## Example 6.5: Confidence Interval for Predicted College GPA

```{julia}
#=
use "data/gpa2.dta", clear
gen hsize2=hsize*hsize
reg colgpa sat hsperc hsize hsize2

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =     4,137
-------------+----------------------------------   F(4, 4132)      =    398.02
       Model |  499.030504         4  124.757626   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  1295.16517     4,132  .313447524   R-squared       =    0.2781
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.2774
       Total |  1794.19567     4,136  .433799728   Root MSE        =    .55986

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      colgpa | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         sat |   .0014925   .0000652    22.89   0.000     .0013646    .0016204
      hsperc |  -.0138558    .000561   -24.70   0.000    -.0149557   -.0127559
       hsize |  -.0608815   .0165012    -3.69   0.000    -.0932328   -.0285302
      hsize2 |   .0054603   .0022698     2.41   0.016     .0010102    .0099104
       _cons |   1.492652   .0753414    19.81   0.000     1.344942    1.640362
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
gpa2 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("gpa2"))
gpa2.hsize2 = gpa2.hsize .* gpa2.hsize
m65 = stata_regress(gpa2; y = :colgpa, x = [:sat, :hsperc, :hsize, :hsize2])
```

```{julia}
#=
Predicted college GPA at sat=1200, hsperc=30, hsize=5, hsize2=25
display _b[_cons]+_b[sat]*1200+_b[hsperc]*30+_b[hsize]*5+_b[hsize2]*25
2.7000755
=#
# coefficient order: (Intercept), sat, hsperc, hsize, hsize2
x0 = [1.0, 1200.0, 30.0, 5.0, 25.0]
Printf.@printf("predicted colgpa = %.7g\n", LinearAlgebra.dot(bvec(m65), x0))
```

Book approach: recenter the regressors at the point of interest so the
intercept *is* the prediction and its SE comes straight from the table. Stata's
recentered `_cons = 2.700075 (.0198778)`.

```{julia}
#=
gen sat0=sat-1200
gen hsperc0=hsperc-30
gen hsize0=hsize-5
gen hsize20=hsize2-25
reg colgpa sat0 hsperc0 hsize0 hsize20
       _cons |   2.700075   .0198778   135.83   0.000     2.661104    2.739047
=#
gpa2.sat0    = gpa2.sat    .- 1200
gpa2.hsperc0 = gpa2.hsperc .- 30
gpa2.hsize0  = gpa2.hsize  .- 5
gpa2.hsize20 = gpa2.hsize2 .- 25
m65c = stata_regress(gpa2; y = :colgpa, x = [:sat0, :hsperc0, :hsize0, :hsize20])
```

Another approach: predict directly, with `stdp` (SE of the mean prediction).
Stata lists `stdp = .0198778`, `xb = 2.700075`, CI `[2.661115, 2.739036]`.

```{julia}
#=
predict colgpahat in 4138/4138, stdp   -> .0198778
predict colgpahatt in 4138/4138, xb    -> 2.700075
list -> .0198778   2.661115   2.700075   2.739036
=#
predict_ci(m65, x0);   # stdp: mean-prediction SE
```

The main fit matches Stata exactly (`sat = .0014925`, `hsize2 = .0054603`,
`R² = 0.2781`). The point prediction is `2.7000755`; the recentered intercept
gives the same value with SE `.0198778`, and `predict_ci` returns identical
`xb`, `stdp` and 95% CI (`[2.66111, 2.73904]`).

## Example 6.6: Confidence Interval for Future College GPA

Same estimated equation, but the interval now uses `stdf` — the SE of a
*forecast* for an individual, which adds the error variance σ². Stata lists
`stdf = .5602166`, CI `[1.602051, 3.7981]`.

```{julia}
#=
predict cc in 4138/4138, stdf          -> .5602166
predict colgpahatt in 4138/4138, xb    -> 2.700075
list -> .5602166   1.602051   2.700075   3.7981
=#
s2 = sigma2_of(m65, gpa2, :colgpa)
Printf.@printf("sigma^2 (Residual MS) = %.7g\n", s2)
predict_ci(m65, x0; forecast = true, sigma2 = s2);   # stdf: forecast SE
```

`sigma^2` recovers Stata's Residual MS (`.313447524`), and the forecast SE
`stdf = .5602166` with 95% CI `[1.60205, 3.79810]` reproduces the published
Example 6.6 numbers to the printed digits.

## Example 6.7: Predicting CEO Salaries

```{julia}
#=
use "data/ceosal2.dta", clear
reg lsalary lsales lmktval ceoten

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =       177
-------------+----------------------------------   F(3, 173)       =     26.91
       Model |  20.5672434         3  6.85574779   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  44.0789697       173  .254791732   R-squared       =    0.3182
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.3063
       Total |  64.6462131       176  .367308029   Root MSE        =    .50477

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     lsalary | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      lsales |   .1628545   .0392421     4.15   0.000     .0853995    .2403094
     lmktval |    .109243   .0495947     2.20   0.029     .0113545    .2071315
      ceoten |   .0117054   .0053261     2.20   0.029      .001193    .0222178
       _cons |   4.503795   .2572344    17.51   0.000     3.996073    5.011517
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
ceosal2 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("ceosal2"))
m67 = stata_regress(ceosal2; y = :lsalary, x = [:lsales, :lmktval, :ceoten])
```

```{julia}
#=
Predicted salary
display _b[_cons]+_b[lsales]*log(5000)+_b[lmktval]*log(10000)+_b[ceoten]*10
7.0140771
=#
lhat = bget(m67, "(Intercept)") + bget(m67, "lsales") * log(5000) +
       bget(m67, "lmktval") * log(10000) + bget(m67, "ceoten") * 10
Printf.@printf("predicted lsalary = %.7g\n", lhat)
```

```{julia}
#=
predict lsal, xb
gen mhat=exp(lsal)
reg salary mhat, noconstant
        mhat |   1.116857   .0470953    23.71   0.000     1.023912    1.209801
=#
ceosal2.lsal = FixedEffectModels.predict(m67, ceosal2)
ceosal2.mhat = exp.(ceosal2.lsal)
m67b = stata_regress(ceosal2; y = :salary, x = [:mhat], nocons = true)
```

```{julia}
#=
Predicted salary (retransformed)
display _b[mhat]*exp(7.013)
1240.8082
=#
Printf.@printf("predicted salary = %.7g\n", bget(m67b, "mhat") * exp(7.013))
```

The log-salary equation reproduces Stata (`lsales = .1628545`, `R² = 0.3182`),
the log prediction is `7.0140771`, the Duan-style scaling regression through the
origin gives `mhat = 1.116857 (.0470953)`, and the retransformed salary
prediction `1240.8082` all match the published numbers.

## Example 6.8: Predicting CEO Salaries

```{julia}
#=
use "data/ceosal2.dta", clear
reg salary sales mktval ceoten

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =       177
-------------+----------------------------------   F(3, 173)       =     14.53
       Model |  12230632.6         3  4076877.52   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  48535332.2       173  280551.053   R-squared       =    0.2013
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.1874
       Total |  60765964.7       176  345261.163   Root MSE        =    529.67

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      salary | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       sales |   .0190191   .0100561     1.89   0.060    -.0008294    .0388676
      mktval |   .0234003   .0094826     2.47   0.015     .0046839    .0421167
      ceoten |   12.70337   5.618052     2.26   0.025     1.614616    23.79211
       _cons |   613.4361   65.23685     9.40   0.000     484.6735    742.1987
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m68 = stata_regress(ceosal2; y = :salary, x = [:sales, :mktval, :ceoten])
```

The levels specification reproduces Stata exactly (`sales = .0190191`,
`mktval = .0234003`, `ceoten = 12.70337`, `R² = 0.2013`, `F(3,173) = 14.53`).
